subject: Physics
q: $5$ moles of unknown gas is heated at constant volume from $10 \, °C$ to $20 \, °C$. The molar specific heat of this gas at constant pressure $c_p = 8$ cal/mol.°C and $R = 8.36$ J/mol.°C. The change in the internal energy of the gas is _______ calorie.
question: $5$ moles of unknown gas is heated at constant volume from $10 \, °C$ to $20 \, °C$. The molar specific heat of this gas at constant pressure $c_p = 8$ cal/mol.°C and $R = 8.36$ J/mol.°C. The change in the internal energy of the gas is _______ calorie.
solution:
Given $n = 5$ moles, $\Delta T = 20^{\circ}\text{C} - 10^{\circ}\text{C} = 10^{\circ}\text{C}$.

The molar specific heat at constant pressure is $c_p = 8 \text{ cal/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}$.

The universal gas constant is $R = 8.36 \text{ J/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}$.

Converting $R$ into calories, we use $1 \text{ cal} = 4.18 \text{ J}$:

$R = \dfrac{8.36}{4.18} \text{ cal/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C} = 2 \text{ cal/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}$

Using Mayer's relation, the molar specific heat at constant volume is:

$c_v = c_p - R = 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ cal/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}$

The change in internal energy of the gas is given by:

$\Delta U = n c_v \Delta T$

$\Delta U = 5 \times 6 \times 10 = 300 \text{ cal}$

Answer: $300$

subject: Physics
q: In an adiabatic process, which of the following statements is true?
question: In an adiabatic process, which of the following statements is true?
options: The molar heat capacity is infinite
Work done by the gas equals the increase in internal energy
The molar heat capacity is zero
The internal energy of the gas decreases as the temperature increases
solution:
For adiabatic process, $\mathrm{dQ}=0$
$\therefore$ Molar heat capacity $=0$
$\because \mathrm{dQ}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{dU}=-\mathrm{dW}$
Also $\mathrm{dU}=\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2} \mathrm{nRdT}$
$\therefore$ Only option (3) is correct.

question: A thermodynamic system is taken through cyclic process. The total work done in the process is :
solution:
The work done is given by the area of the PV graph of the loop.

W=12VE-VC(PD-PC)=2×3002 J

=300 J

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: In an adiabatic process, change in internal energy of a gas is equal to work done on/ by the gas in the process.
Statement 2: The temperature of a gas remains constant in an adiabatic process.
question:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: In an adiabatic process, change in internal energy of a gas is equal to work done on/ by the gas in the process.
Statement 2: The temperature of a gas remains constant in an adiabatic process.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is a correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution:
In an adiabatic process, $\delta \mathrm{H}=0$ And according to first law of thermodynamics
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \delta H=\delta U+W \\
& \therefore W=-\delta U
\end{aligned}
$$

question: The P-V diagram of a diatomic ideal gas system going under cyclic process as shown in figure. The work done during an adiabatic process CD is (use γ=1.4) :

subject: Physics
q: A monoatomic gas is compressed from a volume of 2 m3 to a volume of 1 m3 at a constant pressure of 100 N m2. Then it is heated at constant volume by supplying 150 J of energy. As a result, the internal energy of the gas
question: A monoatomic gas is compressed from a volume of 2 m3 to a volume of 1 m3 at a constant pressure of 100 N m2. Then it is heated at constant volume by supplying 150 J of energy. As a result, the internal energy of the gas
solution:
Isobaric process with Q=0

∴ 0=W+ΔU

∴ 0=PΔV+ΔU

∴ ΔU=-PΔV

      =-1001-2 J

      ΔU=100 J

Constant volume with Q=150 J

Q=0+ΔU

∴ ΔU2=150J

∴ ΔU=ΔU1+ΔU2

               = + 2 5 0 J

Hence, internal energy increases by 250 J

q: Two carnot engines <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> are operated in series. The first one, <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> receives heat at <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>600</mn><mi>K</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and rejects to a reservoir at temperature <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The second engine <math><mi>B</mi></math> receives heat rejected by the first engine and, in turn, rejects to a heat reservoir at <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>400</mn><mi>K</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Calculate the temperature <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> if the work outputs of the two engines are equal:
question: Two carnot engines <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> are operated in series. The first one, <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> receives heat at <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>600</mn><mi>K</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and rejects to a reservoir at temperature <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The second engine <math><mi>B</mi></math> receives heat rejected by the first engine and, in turn, rejects to a heat reservoir at <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>400</mn><mi>K</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Calculate the temperature <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> if the work outputs of the two engines are equal:
solution:
W=Q1-Q2=Q2-Q3

⇒Q1Q2+Q3Q2=2

T1T2+T3T2=2

T2=T1+T32=500K

subject: Physics
q: A reversible heat engine converts one-fourth of the heat input into work. When the temperature of the sink is reduced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>52</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> its efficiency is doubled. The temperature in Kelvin of the source will be ___ .
question: A reversible heat engine converts one-fourth of the heat input into work. When the temperature of the sink is reduced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>52</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> its efficiency is doubled. The temperature in Kelvin of the source will be ___ .
solution:
Efficiency of reversible engine is η=14=WQin=1-T2 T1

From above, T2 T1=34

When the temperature of the sink is reduced by 52 K then its efficiency is doubled.

Then, T2-52 T1=12

⇒T2 T1-52 T1=12⇒34-52 T1=12⇒52 T1=34-12=14⇒T1=52×4=208 K

subject: Physics
q: The volume of an ideal gas $(\gamma=1.5)$ is changed adiabatically from 5 litres to 4 litres. The ratio of initial pressure to final pressure is:
question: The volume of an ideal gas $(\gamma=1.5)$ is changed adiabatically from 5 litres to 4 litres. The ratio of initial pressure to final pressure is:
solution:
For Adiabatic process
$\begin{aligned} & P_i V_i=P_f V_f^\gamma \\ & P_i(5)^{1.5}=P_f(4)^{1.5} \\ & \frac{P_i}{P_f}=\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{4}{5} \cdot\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{8}{5 \sqrt{5}}\end{aligned}$

(Take $\pi=3.14$)
question:
An ideal gas has undergone through the cyclic process as shown in the figure. Work done by the gas in the entire cycle is ______ $\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~J}$.
(Take $\pi=3.14$)
solution:
Area of circle, $\mathrm{W}=\frac{\pi}{4} \mathrm{~d}_1 \mathrm{~d}_2$
$\mathrm{W}=\frac{\pi}{4}(500-300) \times 10^3(350-150) \times 10^{-6}$
W = 31.4 Joule
$\mathrm{W}=314 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{Joule}$

subject: Physics
q: An electric appliance supplies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>min</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, heat to the system. If the system delivers a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. How long it would take to increase the internal energy by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>?
question: An electric appliance supplies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>min</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, heat to the system. If the system delivers a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. How long it would take to increase the internal energy by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>?
solution:
Recall the formula of the first law of thermodynamics in terms of change in internal energy and work done by the system, ΔU=Q-W, given, ΔU=2500 J, Q=600060×t=100t  J and W=P×t=90t, 
⇒2500 J=100t J-90t J⇒t=2.5×102 s.

subject: Physics
q:
Density of water at $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are $1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and $998 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ respectively. The increase in internal energy of 4 kg of water when it is heated from $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
(specific heat capacity of water $=4.2 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}$. and 1 atmospheric pressure $=10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$)
question:
Density of water at $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are $1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and $998 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ respectively. The increase in internal energy of 4 kg of water when it is heated from $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
(specific heat capacity of water $=4.2 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}$. and 1 atmospheric pressure $=10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$)
solution:
Heat absorbed by water: $Q = mc\Delta T = 4 \times 4200 \times 16 = 268800$ J.
Volume change: $V_1 = 4/1000 = 0.004$ m³ at 4°C, $V_2 = 4/998 ≈ 0.00401$ m³ at 20°C.
Work done by water at constant pressure: $W = P_{atm}\Delta V = 10^5 \times 0.00001 = 0.8$ J.
By first law of thermodynamics: $\Delta U = Q - W = 268800 - 0.8 = 268799.2$ J

subject: Physics
q: A gas is compressed adiabatically, which one of the following statement is NOT true?
question: A gas is compressed adiabatically, which one of the following statement is NOT true?
options: There is no heat supplied to the system
There is no change in the internal energy
The change in the internal energy is equal to the work done on the gas
solution:
In adiabatic process,

∆Q=0 and ∆U≠0

Using the first law of thermodynamics, ∆Q=∆U+W

⇒∆U=-W W is the work done by the gas, so -W will be the work done on the gas

As the gas is compressed

W <0

Therefore, ∆U >0 and hence ∆T >0.

wQ2=Q1-Q2Q2=T1-T2T2

q: A heat engine operates between a cold reservoir at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and a hot reservoir at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. It takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of heat from the hot reservoir and delivers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of heat to the cold reservoir in a cycle. The minimum temperature of the hot reservoir has to be
question: A heat engine operates between a cold reservoir at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and a hot reservoir at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. It takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of heat from the hot reservoir and delivers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of heat to the cold reservoir in a cycle. The minimum temperature of the hot reservoir has to be
solution: Recall the formula of efficiency of heat engine in terms of work done and heat supplied to the source, η=WQH=1-TLTH, here TL & TH, is the temperature of the sink and source⇒60300=1-400TH⇒TH=500 K.

subject: Physics
q:
Match List-I with List-II.
$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{(A)} & \text{Isothermal} & \text{(I)} & \Delta \mathrm{W} \text{(work done)} =0 \\ \hline \text{(B)} & \text{Adiabatic} & \text{(II)} & \Delta \mathrm{Q} \text{(supplied heat)} =0 \\ \hline \text{(C)} & \text{Isobaric} & \text{(III)} & \begin{array}{l}\Delta \mathrm{U} \text{(change in internal} \\ \text{energy} \neq 0\end{array} \\ \hline \text{(D)} & \text{Isochoric} & \text{(IV)} & \Delta \mathrm{U}=0 \\ \hline\end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List-I with List-II.
$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{(A)} & \text{Isothermal} & \text{(I)} & \Delta \mathrm{W} \text{(work done)} =0 \\ \hline \text{(B)} & \text{Adiabatic} & \text{(II)} & \Delta \mathrm{Q} \text{(supplied heat)} =0 \\ \hline \text{(C)} & \text{Isobaric} & \text{(III)} & \begin{array}{l}\Delta \mathrm{U} \text{(change in internal} \\ \text{energy} \neq 0\end{array} \\ \hline \text{(D)} & \text{Isochoric} & \text{(IV)} & \Delta \mathrm{U}=0 \\ \hline\end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
(A) Isothermal $\rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{T}=0 \rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{U}=0$ (IV)
(B) Adiabatic $\rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{Q}=0$ (II)
(C) Isobaric $\rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{P}=0 \rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{U} \neq 0$ (III)
(D) Isochoric $\rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{V}=0 \rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{~W}=0(\mathrm{I})$

subject: Physics
q: The pressure and volume of an ideal gas are related as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>V</mi><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>K</mi></math> (Constant). The work done when the gas is taken from state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> to state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
question: The pressure and volume of an ideal gas are related as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>V</mi><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>K</mi></math> (Constant). The work done when the gas is taken from state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> to state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
solution:
For PVx= constant, work done by the gas is given by, 

W=nRΔT1−x=P2V2-P1V11−x.

Here x=32, therefore we can write

∴W=P2V2−P1V1−12

=2P1V1−P2V2

subject: Physics
q: Heat energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>735</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> is given to a diatomic gas allowing the gas to expand at constant pressure. Each gas molecule rotates around an internal axis but do not oscillate. The increase in the internal energy of the gas will be:
question: Heat energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>735</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> is given to a diatomic gas allowing the gas to expand at constant pressure. Each gas molecule rotates around an internal axis but do not oscillate. The increase in the internal energy of the gas will be:
solution:
For isobaric process and diatomic gas we can write, ΔQ=nCPΔT=735 J

⇒ n7R2ΔT=735 J⇒nRΔT=210

Now internal energy will be,

ΔU=nCVΔT=n5R2ΔT=52×210

=525 J

subject: Physics
q:
Given are statements for certain thermodynamic variables,
(A) Internal energy, volume (V) and mass (M) are extensive variables.
(B) Pressure (P), temperature (T) and density ( $\rho$ ) are intensive variables.
(C) Volume (V), temperature (T) and density ( $\rho$ ) are intensive variables.
(D) Mass (M), temperature (T) and internal energy are extensive variables.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given are statements for certain thermodynamic variables,
(A) Internal energy, volume (V) and mass (M) are extensive variables.
(B) Pressure (P), temperature (T) and density ( $\rho$ ) are intensive variables.
(C) Volume (V), temperature (T) and density ( $\rho$ ) are intensive variables.
(D) Mass (M), temperature (T) and internal energy are extensive variables.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Extensive variables depend on size and amount of system.
Extensive : Volume, mass, internal energy
Intensive : Pressure, temperature, density

subject: Physics
q:
Heat is given to an ideal gas in an isothermal process.

A. Internal energy of the gas will decrease.

B. Internal energy of the gas will increase.

C. Internal energy of the gas will not change.

D. The gas will do positive work.

E. The gas will do negative work.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Heat is given to an ideal gas in an isothermal process.

A. Internal energy of the gas will decrease.

B. Internal energy of the gas will increase.

C. Internal energy of the gas will not change.

D. The gas will do positive work.

E. The gas will do negative work.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
From the first law of thermodynamics, heat supplied to the system Q, change in internal energy of the system ∆U and work done by the system W are related as:

Q=∆U+W

Since the process is isothermal, the temperature of the system is constant and therefore ∆U=0 (i.e., internal energy is constant). 

⇒Q=W

As heat is supplied to the system, Q is positive and therefore W is positive i.e., gas will do positive work.

Hence, correct options are C and D.

subject: Physics
q: A monoatomic gas at pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is suddenly compressed to one eighth of its original volume. The final pressure at constant entropy will be
question: A monoatomic gas at pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is suddenly compressed to one eighth of its original volume. The final pressure at constant entropy will be
solution:
Change in entropy is given by dS=dQT. Since, the process is dS=0, dQ=0. Therefore, constant entropy means the process is adiabatic.

PVγ=constant

⇒P1V1γ=P2V2γ

For a monatomic gas γ=53

⇒P1V153=P2V1853

⇒P1V153=P2 V15332

⇒P2=32P1

subject: Physics
q:
The initial pressure and volume of an ideal gas are P0 and V0. The final pressure of the gas when the gas is suddenly compressed to volume V04 will be:

(Given γ= ratio of specific heats at constant pressure and at constant volume.)
question:
The initial pressure and volume of an ideal gas are P0 and V0. The final pressure of the gas when the gas is suddenly compressed to volume V04 will be:

(Given γ= ratio of specific heats at constant pressure and at constant volume.)
solution:
As the gas is suddenly compressed the process is adiabatic.

So, PVγ=constant

⇒P0V0γ=P·V04γ

⇒P=4γ·P0

subject: Physics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is the efficiency of an engine at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>447</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>147</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is the efficiency at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>947</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>47</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is the efficiency of an engine at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>447</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>147</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is the efficiency at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>947</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>47</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>η</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be
solution:
The efficiency of an engine is given by η=1-TLTH. Here, TL and TH are in Kelvin scale.

⇒η1=1-147+273447+273=1-420720

⇒η1=300720

And η2=1-47+273947+273=1-3201220

⇒η2=9001220

⇒η1η2=300720×1220900=12272×3

⇒η1η2=0.56

q: One mole of an ideal monatomic gas is compressed isothermally in a rigid vessel to double its pressure at room temperature, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . The magnitude of work done on the gas will be:
question: One mole of an ideal monatomic gas is compressed isothermally in a rigid vessel to double its pressure at room temperature, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . The magnitude of work done on the gas will be:
solution: Work done on gas<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mi>n</mi><mi>R</mi><mi>T</mi><mi>ln</mi><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi><mrow><mo>(</mo><mn>300</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><mi>ln</mi><mrow><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>300</mn><mi>R</mi><mi>ln</mi><mn>2</mn></math>.

subject: Physics
q: At a certain temperature, the degrees of freedom per molecule for gas is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math>. The gas performs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of work when it expands under constant pressure. The amount of heat absorbed by the gas will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: At a certain temperature, the degrees of freedom per molecule for gas is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math>. The gas performs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of work when it expands under constant pressure. The amount of heat absorbed by the gas will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
For constant pressure process,

Work done is given by W=nRΔT=150 J, here, R is gas constant.

Now, for above process, f=8.

So, specific heat at constant pressure is CP=1+f2R.

Amount of heat adsorbed by the gas is 

Q=nCPRΔT=f2+1nRΔT=82+1150=750 J

subject: Physics
q: Using the given $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{V}$ diagram, the work done by an ideal gas along the path ABCD is :
question: Using the given $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{V}$ diagram, the work done by an ideal gas along the path ABCD is :
solution: Area under graph will be magnitude of graph and being counterclockwise graph it would be negative $\begin{aligned} & \text { Area }=2 P_0 \times V_0+P_0 V_0=3 P_0 V_0 \\ & \mathrm{~W}=-3 P_0 V_0 \end{aligned}$
